data.title: Amazon: It's Never Too Late To Buy
data.description:
Updating My Thesis On AMZN

My most recent article on Amazon.com, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMZN) stock came out in mid-July, and it was a preview piece on the then-upcoming fiscal Q2 print, which I thought should have led to AMZN's rally continuation if the firm beat. It happened eventually - Amazon beat the consensus for Q2. But we've been seeing Amazon's relative lagging compared to the S&P 500 index (SPY) (SP500) since the earnings release date.

I think we can't blame the recent Q2 results themselves, as they didn't disappoint - AMZN's correction is likely just a temporary thing that should eventually smooth out over time. This is my base case assumption regarding Amazon, so this is where my updated title comes from - it's never too late to buy AMZN.

Although the stock might indeed have quite high valuation multiples right now at first glance, I think this premium is reasonable and it's there to stay for the foreseeable future, thanks to the existing moat and ever-growing business verticals, especially when we're talking about the AI-related verticals within AWS. So, like in the past few cases, I'm reiterating my long-term "Buy" rating on AMZN.

Why Do I Think So?

Amazon's released quarterly results were once again better than expected on all fronts, as far as I see it. Their consolidated quarterly sales of ~$167.7 billion (+13.3% YoY) beat the consensus estimate by over $5.6 billion or 3.46% (that's the strongest beating momentum in recent years, based on Seeking Alpha data). And their adjusted EPS came in at ~$1.68 (+33.3% YoY), beating the consensus by a massive 27.19%, according to Seeking Alpha. The magnitude of their double-beat wasn't just a one-off event for Amazon. And the management seemed quite confident on the post-Q2 call. Otherwise, we wouldn't see their Q3 revenue guidance climbing to the range of $174-179.5 billion, which obviously triggered a widespread Wall Street analysts' reaction (see the earnings revisions).

Few analysts expected the level of Amazon's core retail comeback in Q2, but it actually happened. The firm reported that this year's Prime Day set a few historical records for sales, items sold, and new Prime sign-ups, and what was even more importantly, in my view, this year's Prime Day proved that Amazon can greatly leverage its existing ecosystem in such times.

The margins were higher than most analysts were pricing in initially, and the "efficiency flywheel" that we've been hearing about for the past few earnings calls is still leading to tangible gains on the bottom line. According to the updated commentary of the Q2 call, by increasing the use of direct shipping lanes, Amazon effectively "reduced the average distance packages travel by ~12%" and lowered handling touches per unit by ~15%, so their EBIT margins (in both NA and International segments) are higher and more sustainable now versus the industry's norms. And while being bigger in absolute terms, Amazon keeps gaining a massive competitive advantage on this incremental "efficiency, efficiency", which is impossible to beat to date, in my opinion.

What Amazon proved in Q2 was the fact that it can largely mitigate the tariff impact that everyone on the Street was concerned about lately. The diversity of Amazon's third-party marketplace has given it a well-managed buffer that basically absorbed cost pressures, and it did so way more effectively than its smaller peers. Going forward, I see no major hits to this competitive advantage any time soon.

If costs end up being higher, who will absorb them, but what we can share is what we've seen thus far, which is that through the first half of the year, we haven't yet seen diminishing demand nor price is meaningfully appreciating. We also have such diversity of sellers in our marketplace over 2 million sellers in total with different strategies of whether to pass on higher cost to consumers, the customers are advantaged shopping at Amazon because they're more likely to find lower prices on the items they care about.

Source: AMZN's Q2 earnings call.

When looking at AMZN's forward P/E of over 30x, one can think that the stock has become pricier compared to the past few years. But I don't quite agree with that.

The business segment that is mostly responsible for the valuation premium in Amazon's stock price - the firm's AWS segment - has grown its top line by 17.5% YoY to a massive $123 billion ARR. While I agree with skeptics saying that there are smaller peers growing faster, the ecosystem of multiple AI-related layers that AWS has grown into is what makes a difference. It's not just a cloud provider anymore.

With the new Trainium2 chip at the bottom layer of the stack and Amazon's eventual reliance reduction on third-party suppliers in the future, the firm can a) move faster in innovations like Strands and AgentCore to create and deploy AI agents securely at scale, and b) let them monetize the new offerings quicker and to a higher extent than some investors may assume today. I don't know about this catalyst's timing, but once the Street realizes this potential, I think this should give AMZN investors more hope for valuation premium sustainability.

We see that AWS's EBIT margin dipped from the previous Q1 peaks, but it was largely expected, and I'm now looking forward to seeing the previous AI-driven CapEx translate into even higher efficiency and new marginality peaks next year.

By the way, speaking about AMZN's exact valuation levels, I noted above that the statement of higher-than-before multiples is only partially true. In reality, we see that the multiples are becoming reasonable now - at least on a log scale, we see Amazon stock trading close to its historical bottom in terms of price-to-earnings ratios:

The premium in valuation still exists, and we can't call AMZN a cheap stock when we simply use conventional metrics such as TTM EV/EBITDA or P/E ratios and compare them with the industry norms. Amazon is not a "normal company" in this sense, which is why Seeking Alpha's quant system shows a "D-" grade for the stock's valuation.

But I see "reasonableness" in the way the market prices the stock to date in the firm's future operational scaling. We can see it clearly when we take a look at Amazon's 2-year forward EV/EBITDA ratio, which is now only at 10.9x, and it's over 3x less than the long-term average for Amazon, based on YCharts:

So, the growth factor that explains the "reasonableness" of AMZN's valuation doesn't go anywhere. Over the next 5 years, AMZN should grow its EPS with a CAGR of over 19% annually, according to Seeking Alpha:

But the past quarterly beats tell us that Amazon may be able to show much more than what the consensus usually prices in:

Assuming a 15-20% premium on today's consensus for FY2026 EPS and putting a P/E exit multiple on the resulting figure, I get a price target of ~$267.2/share. That's an upside of 15-16% to the current stock price, so that's the reason for my bullish upgrade today.

But I see in some sell-side reports that analysts come to even brighter conclusions regarding AMZN's fair value. The analysts at Argus Research (proprietary source, August 2025) updated their models, basically assuming a similar trend in constant premium re-pricing for years to come, and I see that they have a much higher upside target ahead:

We believe that AMZN merits a premium to its historical EV/EBITDA given the company's growth in high-end offerings such as Prime and Prime Video; superior growth in AWS, digital advertising, and subscription services; and unmatched volume and vendor leverage. Our historical comparable valuation is in the low $300s, in a slightly rising trend and above current levels.

Peer indicated value is in the $270s, rising on the tech recovery. Using our two- and three-stage discounted free cash flow model, we calculate a value in the $400 range, well above current prices. Based on our historical comparable analysis, peer indicated value, and discounted free cash flow valuation, our blended model points to a value around $350, well above current prices.

Source: Argus Research (proprietary source, August 2025), emphasis added by the author

But even if we take my findings as the base case scenario, we'll see that AMZN is worth buying still (even at today's highs).

Where Can I Be Wrong?

The risks are there, and as I wrote in my previous bullish takes, I may be too optimistic on the firm's CapEx (and the future ROIC) and the EBITDA contribution over the next few years. If AWS loses its dominance somehow, the money Amazon burns today for CapEx won't lead to a meaningful positive impact, so as a result, the value erosion may be massive in this case.

Another risk comes from my valuation analysis. I added 15% to the current EPS estimate for FY2026, and I also projected the same P/E exit multiple, so if AMZN trades at 25x and delivers an FY2026 EPS of $7.58, then its price target would be close to $189.5/share, which is an 18% downside potential to the current price.

Goldman Sachs' analysts (proprietary source) in their post-Q2 update showed a price target of $220/share, and it's also below the current price. This makes my findings too optimistic.

The Bottom Line

Despite the risks, there are plenty of other projects like Project Kuiper (AMZN's satellite internet initiative) or their Zoox robotaxi service (recently launched in Las Vegas), which theoretically add to and justify the existing valuation premium. The current forward 30x earnings multiple may turn out to be even lower than I'm taking for today's analysis, and it gives an additional upside layer to my base case 15-16% upside projection. That's why I'm leaving my "Buy" rating in place for now.

Thank you for reading!
